name: aeroMorph
short_description: A design, simulation, and fabrication pipeline for heat-sealed inflatable structures that fold into programmable shapes when pressurized with air.
long_description: aeroMorph is a research prototype from the MIT Media Lab Tangible Media Group that introduces a universal bending mechanism for creating programmable shape-change in inextensible materials such as paper, plastics, and fabrics. A software tool generates the heat-sealing pattern for a target geometry, simulates its transformation, and exports digital fabrication files, while a custom CNC-mountable heat-sealing head fabricates the inflatables. As physical AI and interaction design, it advances shape-changing interfaces and pneumatic actuation as a low-cost, lightweight medium for responsive wearables, toys, and packaging that move and reconfigure on demand.
ai_note: No AI/ML is involved; aeroMorph is a pneumatic shape-change fabrication technique driven by a geometric folding-pattern generator and air pressure, not learned models.

images.caption: Heat-sealed inflatable structures from aeroMorph folding into programmed shapes when pressurized.
images.alt_text: Flat sheets of paper and plastic with sealed seam patterns that bend and fold into three-dimensional forms when inflated with air.

sources.title: aeroMorph
sources.publisher: MIT Media Lab, Tangible Media Group

sources.title: aeroMorph - Heat-sealing Inflatable Shape-change Materials for Interaction Design
